editableNewDialogue.text: If we wanted to do a street party that isn’t tied to the royals, what themes work well?

A few that usually land well: a “summer picnic” day, a local history/heritage theme, a sports day (tennis-in-the-road style games), a harvest/food theme with a shared table, or a charity fundraiser day. You can also anchor it to something practical like a community clean-up followed by a potluck.
What are some specific occasions people use as an excuse?

Common non-royal anchors include: the street’s “founding”/first appearance on old maps (even if approximate), a centenary of a local building (school, church hall, park), an annual summer weekend (e.g., “first Saturday in July” tradition), national events like VE Day anniversaries, big sporting finals, or a local festival week. Some places do a “Neighbour Day” in late spring just as a recurring meet-your-neighbours event.
Anything we should watch out for when planning it?

Main things are permissions, safety, and noise. If you’re closing a road, you typically need a Temporary Traffic Regulation Order (or your council’s street-party process), plus barriers/signage and a plan for emergency access. Also think about public liability insurance (sometimes optional, sometimes recommended), food hygiene if you’re selling food, and keeping music within reasonable hours. If you tell me whether it’s a full road closure or just a pavement/front-garden gathering, I can suggest a tighter checklist.
Could we do something tied to a historical date even if it’s not a bank holiday?

Yes—plenty of communities pick a meaningful local date. For example, a “street birthday” based on when the first houses were completed, or an anniversary of a nearby park opening. (Unrelated example: the Eiffel Tower opened to the public in 1889, and Paris has used that as a recurring cultural hook for exhibitions—local dates can work the same way at street scale.)

I’m putting together a little get‑together for our neighbours this summer and I’m trying to work out what we can do without getting into the whole road‑closure paperwork again—last time we did that it took ages.

If you want to avoid road-closure admin, you could frame it as something that stays on private drives/front gardens or a “meet-and-mingle” on the pavement without blocking through access. Themes that work well include seasonal celebrations, charity coffee mornings, a welcome event for new neighbours, or marking a local milestone (e.g., a street anniversary), depending on what your council guidelines allow.
Earlier I said "my street in england" but I should clarify: I’m not in England—I live in Wales. Same kind of street party idea, though.
Got it—so you’re in Wales, not England. When you said "my street in england" earlier, that was just shorthand, but your location is Wales.
